{
  "decision": "fail",
  "risk": "high",
  "work_stage": "in-service",
  "component_conditions": ["installed-defective", "non-compliant"],
  "percent_complete": 100,

  "confidence": 0.95,
  "event_time": 0.0,
  "rate": 0.0,
  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "GFCI receptacle wired with line and load reversed, defeating load-side protection",
    "Downstream loads on load terminals are not GFCI-protected despite appearing to be on a protected circuit",
    "Installation does not comply with NEC 210.8 GFCI protection requirements for covered locations"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "De-energize circuit and correct line/load wiring at GFCI device",
    "Verify proper GFCI function with test button after rewiring",
    "Confirm downstream receptacles on load side are now GFCI-protected using a GFCI tester",
    "Inspect all GFCI devices on property for similar line/load reversal"
  ],
  "rationale": "Line/load reversal on a GFCI receptacle means the GFCI's internal sensing circuitry is connected to the load-side conductors rather than the supply. The receptacle face may still have power and the test button may appear to function, but downstream loads connected to the load terminals are not protected by the GFCI. This creates a false sense of safety and violates NEC 210.8 which requires GFCI protection for receptacles in covered locations. The condition is an installation defect that renders the protective function ineffective for the protected branch circuit.",
  "references": ["NEC 210.8", "NEC 210.12"]
}
